speaker: interviewer
text: June 1, 1995. After the Israeli left, what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to look up this [INAUDIBLE], this-- the-- the name that the Israeli gave me. And I told him, could you help us to get out of here? He said, yes. It's gonna cost you money. How much, I said. He said, 10,000 crowns. Well, I sold my parents' farms and I converted, I don't know, a million Czech crowns, and I got 1,000 American dollars for it. So that was it for [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, yes, I'll give you 10,000 crowns. And he said, well, you have to get packed and come to the station, and I'll be waiting for you. It was evening, and I-- two detectives were searching the place where I was staying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were searching the place because the guy who sold me the American money was caught and he squealed on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these two civilian policemen searched the house, couldn't find the dollars. And when we came to the station, my brother-in-law and my friend Gus was accompanying us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see these two policemen there in civilian clothes, these two guys who came to look for the dollars. I see them standing there. Oh, my god, they're gonna recognize me and this is all. It's over. We had the baby then. It was on my arm. And the-- the chap who helped us to come up said, I want to go back. I want to go back. These two guys will recognize me. I said, no. There's no way to go back. Get into the train. Get into the train. We got into the train safely with another couple, a Czech fellow with a Jewish lady, Jewish woman. And-- and my wife made a-- a bag, like a sleeping bag, for the baby, for Paul, and the money was in there, sewed in there. And we got to the border, to the German border. And four of us, five of us from the train, got out. It was night. And a guy in high boots, a-- [INAUDIBLE], was waiting for us. He took us into the forest. And it was night, and we slept there. And my wife Inge got a sort of a nervous breakdown. She started to cry. I'm not going. I'm not going. And the guy said, that's what we need, that we thought the baby would be crying but your wife is crying. So we calmed her down. At about 4 o'clock, he goes, get us out Get us out. Get us all out. And we walked a kilometer to the-- to the border. And it was all-- he-- he bribed, I guess, the customs officers. They looked in his suitcase, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German-- the-- the-- the customs, not the customs, the guard, the border guards opened the-- the gates and we were out in a-- in a-- in a truck with this couple, this Czech guy with the Jewish woman. And we came. It was Germany already. Where in Germany were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a small town and we wanted to get to-- to-- [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A city. We got into the train, and the train took us to the city and we got-- saw-- saw American soldiers and the first time in my life I saw Coca-Cola. And then-- and we had the baby and the Czech fellow said, we're in this town, and I have a friend here. He is a Czech fellow who lives with a Jewish woman, with a German woman. I-- we have to find him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have the two women and my baby there and two of us-- what's his name-- and myself went to look for this guy. And we found the place and the German, you know, the houses were bombed and were destroyed. I forgot the name, Dresden, or whatever it was. Were you in East Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in East-- East Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was West Germany. This was West Germany. And he knocked on the door and the woman came to the door and [? Elijah, ?] that was the Czech boy, said that's-- I-- I-- I-- do you know a [INAUDIBLE] Vladimir? She said, yes, he's my boyfriend. Is he here? Yeah, come on in. We were let in. And he said, we are not alone, the two of us. Our wife and Albert's wife and the child is still in the station. It's all right. You can come in. Bring them in, but it's a problem. He said, there are Russian soldiers in the next room sleeping here. So you have to behave very quietly.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the station, picked the wives up. Lo and behold, we come to the station and Paul's little arm is hang-- hanging like this. He couldn't move it. Anyway, we came to this place, and I said well, the baby's arm, what are we to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She goes, you have to see a doctor. So we went to the street with the child, and we knocked on the door. We saw the sign, doctor. We knocked on the door. They opened the door and said, his arm is hanging down. He said, he looked at the arm. We'll go over it. We'll go over it. Take him home. In the morning, he'll be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we slept there, I guess, in one bed, all of us, two beds all of us. Anyway, the boy didn't cry, and in the morning he was OK. He moved his arm. Where did you go from there? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we bought the German passport, the kennkarte, so you could buy milk, and everything was rationed off in Germany. So we were going to the black market buy bread-- bought bread, and there we found out that they are taking Jewish men or families to Israel. And we found out the place where you-- where they assemble you. And the man in charge, he was in uniform. He said, well, I can't help you with the child. He said, you can't go to Israel because there is hardly any place with a child there. I said, we-- but we still want to go to Canada because I have a friend who graduated with me from high school, and he lives in Canada. He-- he wants us to get-- to get to Canada. We come-- we want to go to France because he's gonna send us the papers. Well, OK, so I'll get you from Frankfurt, from this German town. I'll get you with the transport. The rest will go through on the way to Israel, but you get off in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got off in Paris, and there was waiting-- Jewish people were waiting for us like a Blue Cross there. They given us cherries. This was May. It was May. I told you May 28. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gave us cherries and food. And they took us to a hotel, and we stayed there in the hotel a few months until papers came from Canada and the Canadian embassy-- consulate called us. We were examined health-wise and we got the papers, and we got to Canada, to Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada, August the 15th, '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came from Halifax to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Halifax to Toronto. I had to stay on the farm. The light is imitating me. Can we have a pause or something? I'm whacked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that better? We're almost finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't look into the light.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It bothers my eyes. Is it OK this way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, sure, look down. Just to conclude, so you came to Canada,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Canada? What has your occupation been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to stay on the farm for a year as laborer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Grimsby on the farm for a year, and then I came to-- to Toronto, and I got a job as a factory laborer. And my wife was a housekeeper in a-- a [? Canon ?] [? Plumtree's ?] household. And his daughter was in charge of the Red Cross, and her husband was in the-- in the Canadian government, Ontario government, with Premier Leslie Frost. His name was [? Tyrrell, ?] Tom [? Tyrrell. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back to school. Did you go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked a year in the factory, then I-- then I was a shipper and bookkeeper for 13 years, six and seven, 13 years. And then I went back to university and got my Canadian teacher's certificate. And I got a job at Bickford Park High School where I stayed one year. Bickford Park-- no, my first job was at George Harvey Secondary School for one year then for 15 years at George-- at Bickford Park High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you have how many-- tell-- the names of your children. You have how many children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul is 48, Judy is 45, and John is 31.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since your retirement, what have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sang in the choirs in the beginning in-- at-- at Beth Sholom Synagogue for three years. Then for two years at the-- at the young men of England, Hebrew Men of England Synagogue. And then somebody heard me in a solo, and they asked me would I be-- they would want to hear me. A new congregation is being formed. And I auditioned for them, and they hired me as a cantor. And I'm 40 years with them as a cantor. It's a-- formed by European Jewish German immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What it is called? What is the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. It's called Habonim Congregational Temple, Congregation Habonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And just to summarize your story, to conclude.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there-- is there anything now that I've forgotten to ask you, anything you would like to add to what you've told us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything you would like to say? Just a concluding statement, how the-- how your experiences and the Holocaust have affected your life. Anything you would like to tell your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the encounter with the major that was in my cousin's house in Prague, that saved my life because I asked him, how come as a German that you want to save Jews? Aren't you afraid for your life if this blows up? And excitingly he said, well, sooner or later everybody has to die. And this was an arrogant question that I dared to ask him, and it may have saved my life because I said, well, the man is drinking and his face is red and I can't trust him. Because the answer he gave me that one has to die anyways, I said maybe I don't want to die. I want to live. And what I'd like to leave back in my expose about my past is that anytime that you want to say the truth that's on your heart, say it. It might be bad, but it might be good. And in my case, it was good. And if you learn to say the truth, say it. It might just be beneficial to you. Instead of giving in and saying, yes, I want-- I want to be saved but you're not 100% sure about somebody who is doing something illegally. Although, if it's-- if it's-- if it's for saving somebody as a friend, I can understand, but to ask money for it, I smelled a rat. And I did the right decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to leave this back to the future that might be as clouded as our past, you never know. Say the truth. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want me to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's my father, Herman, Hungarian [? Armin ?] Feldman, and my mother Selma [? May ?] Eisler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have no idea, a number of years ago. They could have been in their 50s. It's Ingeborg Feldman [? May ?] [? Porgus. ?] She was a young girl, 16. A picture taken somewhere on the farm. Albert Feldman, in Hungarian, [? Baylor, ?] in the Czech, [INAUDIBLE], walking on Wenzelsplatz in Prague just before the war. It's my wedding picture in front of the magistrate in Prague. And present is Inge's sister, Vera, Inga's sister's husband, and my friend from my university studies as a witness. This is the wedding picture. You can see Inge holding the bouquet of flowers. In the background it's me. Then is Vera, Inge's sister, and her husband [INAUDIBLE]. This is taken right after the war. Inge with me walking around a swimming pool in Prague. That's about it. This is me as cantor of Habonim standing in-- on the back, on the yard of the synagogue. And next to me is cantor Henry [? Wineglock ?] who was blowing the shofar for a number of years. He really started with me in 1955 at the congregation. And he's now dead for about five years. He was a famous painter who studied in Paris with Toulouse-Lautrec and all the famous painters, with Chagall, too. And he was a great man and a colleague of mine. This picture has been taken about 25 years ago. It's my family in the backyard of our house on 64 Tavistock Road. Present is Inge, my wife, and Judy, my daughter, and Paul, who is 48, who was 48 on May the 28th. And Judy is 45. her birthday is June the 13th. And John, the young one, who is 31, will be 32 in November. The congregation Habonim arranged a concert where I was participating. And with me, my wife Inge, my late wife Inge. And that's about it. I'm all dressed up for the occasion. The picture is Paul and his wife, [? Leigh ?] [? Catsin, ?] and it's their wedding time. And it happened about either 24 or 25 years ago. It will be August the 16th, it will be either 24 or 25 years. It was in the city here. My daughter Judy Markowitz and her husband [? Alan ?] Marcovitch. Judy was born, was going to be 45 this June the 13th, just a few days before her birthday. And I don't know the year when the picture was taken. It's fairly late, lately. My younger son John. He is a 31 now. The hair is real, but it's not there anymore. My lady friend [? Margie ?] [? Getler. ?] We were together 13 years. And the picture has been taken in West Palm Beach where we were visiting her daughter who lives steadily now for 14 years in Boynton Beach, Florida. So we have free room and board.
